Multi-Agent Automation Swarm

Generated: 2026-07-21 11:32 UTC Methodologies: STRIDE, LINDDUN, PASTA LLM-enhanced: No (not requested)

System Description

A supervisor agent decomposes goals and delegates to specialised worker agents (research, coder, action) that coordinate over a shared blackboard and shared memory, call tools/MCP, and can spawn sub-agents. Modelled for agent-to-agent trust, cascading prompt injection, cross-agent memory poisoning, and unbounded spawning across the OWASP LLM Top 10 + Agentic threats.

Counts below are findings — threats proven by your model. Generic “standard checks” are listed lower down and not counted here.

Data-flow overview

Multi-Agent Automation Swarm has 11 components and 12 data flows across 4 trust zones. Requests enter at Task API and MCP Gateway, data is stored in Embedding Store. 3 trust-boundary crossings, the highest-risk component is Coder Agent (6 critical findings).

Entry points: Task API, MCP GatewayData stores: Embedding StoreExternal deps: —Boundary crossings: 3Unencrypted flows: —
FlowWhy it's riskySeverity
Operator → Task APIcrosses a trust boundaryCritical
Research Agent → Embedding Storecrosses a trust boundaryCritical
Action Agent → MCP Gatewaycrosses a trust boundaryCritical
Risk hotspots: Coder Agent (6C/12H) · Exec Tools (3C/12H) · Action Agent (3C/3H) · Embedding Store (1C/11H)

Flows where untrusted (or less-trusted) input crosses into an internal trust zone. These are the highest-priority validation points in the system — every byte that enters here must be treated as hostile until proven otherwise.

No untrusted-input boundary crossings detected. Either there are no internal zones defined, or no flows enter them from less-trusted zones.

Excessive agency — autonomous agent with write/exec tools and no human review: Action Agent
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Action Agent (ai_agent) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk43/50 · Critical
Damage9/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Action Agent (ai_agent).

📝Description

A fully autonomous agent that can take write/exec actions with no human-in-the-loop can perform unintended or attacker-induced actions at scale (OWASP LLM06 Excessive Agency / Agentic).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Agent'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Action Agent as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Action Agent should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

A01LLM06T3

🔗References

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Coder Agent (ai_agent).

📝Description

An element that executes code or tools without a sandbox risks remote code execution and host compromise if the model is manipulated (OWASP LLM06 / Agentic).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Coder Agent'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Coder Agent as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Coder Agent should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Research Agent → Embedding Store (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Embedding Store (vector_db).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Data tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Application tier' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Research Agent → Embedding Store crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Application tier`.
  2. Issues requests to Embedding Store that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Data tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Embedding Store acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

🗺Framework mapping

preventive

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Supervisor Agent (agent_orchestrator).

📝Description

Agents that act with tools can take unintended or attacker-induced actions (OWASP LLM06 Excessive Agency). Confirm the autonomy level, how privileged the tool access is, and whether high-impact actions require human approval.

🔎Why this fired

Standard check: 'Supervisor Agent' is a agent orchestrator component, which is subject to LLM06. Answer 'autonomy_level / tool_access / human_in_the_loop' to confirm this as a finding or clear it.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Supervisor Agent as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Supervisor Agent should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

On the data flow Operator → Task API (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: oauth, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into Application tier. The receiving component is Task API (api).

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Supervisor Agent → Action Agent (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Action Agent (ai_agent).

📝Description

This call may authenticate the caller but enforces no authorization, so any authenticated caller can invoke it — Broken Access Control / BOLA (OWASP A01 / API1).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Agent' for this flow.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Action Agent as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Action Agent should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

A01API1API5

Prompt injection — untrusted content, no defense: Coder Agent
High STRIDE · Tampering · Coder Agent (ai_agent) CWE-345 finding
▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Coder Agent (ai_agent).

📝Description

This element ingests untrusted content into the model context with no prompt-injection defenses, so attacker-controlled text can override instructions (OWASP LLM01 Prompt Injection).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Coder Agent'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Coder Agent (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Coder Agent processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03LLM01T6

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection ↗Agentic T6 — Intent Breaking & Goal Manipulation ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗

▼
DREAD risk33/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Coder Agent (ai_agent).

📝Description

Model output is consumed downstream without validation or encoding, enabling insecure output handling — injection into tools, code, SQL, or the browser (OWASP LLM05 Improper Output Handling).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Coder Agent'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Coder Agent (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Coder Agent processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

Within component Coder Agent (ai_agent).

📝Description

Model responses are returned without filtering or redaction, so training data, context, secrets or other users' data can leak into output (OWASP LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Coder Agent'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Coder Agent via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For ai_agent, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
No authorization on flow: Supervisor Agent → Coder Agent
High STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Coder Agent (ai_agent) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk33/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Supervisor Agent → Coder Agent (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Coder Agent (ai_agent).

📝Description

This call may authenticate the caller but enforces no authorization, so any authenticated caller can invoke it — Broken Access Control / BOLA (OWASP A01 / API1).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Coder Agent' for this flow.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Coder Agent as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Coder Agent should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

A01API1API5

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗API5:2023 Broken Function Level Authorization ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Trust-boundary crossing without strong authn: Research Agent → Embedding Store
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Embedding Store (vector_db) CWE-287 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Research Agent → Embedding Store (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Embedding Store (vector_db).

📝Description

Flow '' crosses trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Research Agent → Embedding Store crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. Embedding Store accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into Embedding Store, terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.

🗺Framework mapping

A07LLM08T1

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses ↗Agentic T1 — Memory Poisoning ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Cross-boundary input not validated: Research Agent → Embedding Store
High STRIDE · Tampering · Embedding Store (vector_db) CWE-20 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Research Agent → Embedding Store (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Embedding Store (vector_db).

📝Description

Data crossing the trust boundary into 'Data tier' must be treated as untrusted, even if the source is internal. Implicit trust is the most common cause of injection / SSRF / deserialization bugs.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Research Agent → Embedding Store crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to Embedding Store.
  2. Sends payloads with unexpected types, lengths, or special characters (e.g. SQL meta-chars, NUL bytes, control sequences).
  3. Embedding Store processes the input under the assumption that the upstream zone has already validated it — but the boundary changes the security context.
  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into Embedding Store or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever Embedding Store does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

🗺Framework mapping

A03LLM08T1

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses ↗Agentic T1 — Memory Poisoning ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-20 — Improper Input Validation ↗
Cross-boundary data exposure risk: Research Agent → Embedding Store
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Embedding Store (vector_db) CWE-200 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability8/10
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Research Agent → Embedding Store (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Embedding Store (vector_db).

📝Description

Information leaving 'Application tier' into 'Data tier' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Research Agent → Embedding Store crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM08T1

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses ↗Agentic T1 — Memory Poisoning ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Agent memory shared across users: Embedding Store
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Embedding Store (vector_db) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk34/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Embedding Store (vector_db).

📝Description

Memory shared across users/tenants enables cross-boundary data leakage and memory poisoning, where one party's injected content influences another's session (OWASP LLM / Agentic).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Embedding Store'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Embedding Store via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For vector_db, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM08T1LLM04

🔗References

Vector/embedding weakness — no per-tenant access control: Embedding Store
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Embedding Store (vector_db) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Embedding Store (vector_db).

📝Description

The vector store enforces no per-tenant access control, so retrieval can return another tenant's embeddings and is open to embedding inversion and poisoning (OWASP LLM08 Vector & Embedding Weaknesses).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Embedding Store'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Embedding Store via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For vector_db, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM08T1A01API1

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses ↗Agentic T1 — Memory Poisoning ↗A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Model output used without validation: Exec Tools
High STRIDE · Tampering · Exec Tools (llm_tool) CWE-345 finding
▼
DREAD risk32/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Exec Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

Model output is consumed downstream without validation or encoding, enabling insecure output handling — injection into tools, code, SQL, or the browser (OWASP LLM05 Improper Output Handling).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Exec Tools'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Exec Tools (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Exec Tools processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03LLM05

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Model/tool supply chain — unverified provenance: Exec Tools
High STRIDE · Tampering · Exec Tools (llm_tool) CWE-345 finding
▼
DREAD risk32/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Exec Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

This element uses a model, plugin or tool from an unverified or community source, exposing it to backdoored weights, tampered dependencies and malicious plugins (OWASP LLM03 Supply Chain).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Exec Tools'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Exec Tools (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Exec Tools processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

⚔️Attack scenario

📝Description

An agent can call this tool with no authorization check, so a manipulated agent can trigger unauthorized actions — excessive agency via unscoped tool access (OWASP LLM / Agentic).

  1. Attacker authenticates to Exec Tools as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.

Boundary crossing: Application tier → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Action Agent → MCP Gateway (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is MCP Gateway (mcp_server).

📝Description

Flow '' crosses trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Action Agent → MCP Gateway crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. MCP Gateway accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into MCP Gateway, terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.

🔎Why this fired

⚔️Attack scenario

  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into MCP Gateway or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.

Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever MCP Gateway does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. MCP Gateway processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03LLM03A06

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM03:2025 Supply Chain ↗A06:2021 Vulnerable & Outdated Components ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Authentication bypass via credential stuffing
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Operator (user) CWE-287 standard check
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Operator (user).

📝Description

Attackers reuse leaked credentials to impersonate legitimate users.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'user' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker obtains valid credentials via phishing, credential stuffing, or a third-party breach.
  2. Logs in as the legitimate user/entity at Operator.
  3. Performs actions indistinguishable from the real user (auth events look normal).
  4. Damage scales with the impersonated user's permissions; admin accounts cause the most harm.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗

Prompt injection — untrusted content, no defense: Research Agent
High STRIDE · Tampering · Research Agent (ai_agent) CWE-345 finding
▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Research Agent (ai_agent).

📝Description

This element ingests untrusted content into the model context with no prompt-injection defenses, so attacker-controlled text can override instructions (OWASP LLM01 Prompt Injection).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Research Agent'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Research Agent (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Research Agent processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03LLM01T6

🔗References

Model output used without validation: Research Agent
High STRIDE · Tampering · Research Agent (ai_agent) CWE-345 finding
▼
DREAD risk33/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Research Agent (ai_agent).

📝Description

Model output is consumed downstream without validation or encoding, enabling insecure output handling — injection into tools, code, SQL, or the browser (OWASP LLM05 Improper Output Handling).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Research Agent'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Research Agent (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Research Agent processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

A03LLM05

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Sensitive information disclosure — model output not filtered: Research Agent
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Research Agent (ai_agent) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk34/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Research Agent (ai_agent).

📝Description

Model responses are returned without filtering or redaction, so training data, context, secrets or other users' data can leak into output (OWASP LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Research Agent'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Research Agent via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For ai_agent, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Research Agent (ai_agent).

📝Description

This element uses a model, plugin or tool from an unverified or community source, exposing it to backdoored weights, tampered dependencies and malicious plugins (OWASP LLM03 Supply Chain).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Research Agent'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Research Agent (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Research Agent processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

A03LLM03A06

No authorization on flow: Supervisor Agent → Research Agent
High STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Research Agent (ai_agent) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk33/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Supervisor Agent → Research Agent (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Research Agent (ai_agent).

📝Description

This call may authenticate the caller but enforces no authorization, so any authenticated caller can invoke it — Broken Access Control / BOLA (OWASP A01 / API1).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Research Agent' for this flow.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Research Agent as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Research Agent should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

A01API1API5

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗API5:2023 Broken Function Level Authorization ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Untrusted grounding source: Shared Blackboard

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Shared Blackboard (knowledge_base).

📝Description

Grounding/RAG content from untrusted sources can carry indirect prompt injection and poisoned data that the model treats as instructions (OWASP LLM04 Data Poisoning / LLM01).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Shared Blackboard'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Shared Blackboard (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Shared Blackboard processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Shared Blackboard (knowledge_base) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Shared Blackboard (knowledge_base).

📝Description

The vector store enforces no per-tenant access control, so retrieval can return another tenant's embeddings and is open to embedding inversion and poisoning (OWASP LLM08 Vector & Embedding Weaknesses).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Shared Blackboard'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Shared Blackboard via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For knowledge_base, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM08T1A01API1

🔗References

DREAD risk34/50 · High

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Shared Blackboard (knowledge_base).

📝Description

Shared agent memory/embeddings can leak across users/tenants and be poisoned so one party's content influences another's session (OWASP LLM / Agentic). Confirm the memory isolation scope.

🔎Why this fired

Standard check: 'Shared Blackboard' is a knowledge base component, which is subject to LLM04. Answer 'memory_scope' to confirm this as a finding or clear it. · severity lowered: internal, encrypted path with no model evidence of exposure

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Shared Blackboard via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For knowledge_base, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Shared Memory (agent_memory).

📝Description

Memory shared across users/tenants enables cross-boundary data leakage and memory poisoning, where one party's injected content influences another's session (OWASP LLM / Agentic).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Shared Memory'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Shared Memory via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

preventive

The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For agent_memory, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

A02LLM08T1A01API1LLM04

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses ↗Agentic T1 — Memory Poisoning ↗A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗LLM04:2025 Data and Model Poisoning ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
No authorization on flow: Supervisor Agent → Shared Memory
High STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Shared Memory (agent_memory) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk32/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Supervisor Agent → Shared Memory (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Shared Memory (agent_memory).

📝Description

This call may authenticate the caller but enforces no authorization, so any authenticated caller can invoke it — Broken Access Control / BOLA (OWASP A01 / API1).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Shared Memory' for this flow.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Shared Memory as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Shared Memory should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

A01API1API5

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗API5:2023 Broken Function Level Authorization ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Model output used without validation: Supervisor Agent
High STRIDE · Tampering · Supervisor Agent (agent_orchestrator) CWE-345 finding
▼
DREAD risk34/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Supervisor Agent (agent_orchestrator).

📝Description

Model output is consumed downstream without validation or encoding, enabling insecure output handling — injection into tools, code, SQL, or the browser (OWASP LLM05 Improper Output Handling).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Supervisor Agent'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Supervisor Agent (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Supervisor Agent processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03LLM05

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Sensitive information disclosure — model output not filtered: Supervisor Agent
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Supervisor Agent (agent_orchestrator) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Supervisor Agent (agent_orchestrator).

📝Description

Model responses are returned without filtering or redaction, so training data, context, secrets or other users' data can leak into output (OWASP LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Supervisor Agent'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Supervisor Agent via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For agent_orchestrator, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗

High STRIDE · Tampering · Supervisor Agent (agent_orchestrator) CWE-345 standard check
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Supervisor Agent (agent_orchestrator).

📝Description

LLM/agent elements can be steered by attacker-controlled text placed in the model context (OWASP LLM01 Prompt Injection). Confirm whether this ingests untrusted content and whether prompt-injection defenses exist.

🔎Why this fired

Standard check: 'Supervisor Agent' is a agent orchestrator component, which is subject to LLM01. Answer 'ingests_untrusted_content / prompt_injection_defense' to confirm this as a finding or clear it.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Supervisor Agent (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Supervisor Agent processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

A03LLM01T6

Authentication bypass via credential stuffing
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Task API (api) CWE-287 standard check
▼
DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Task API (api).

📝Description

Attackers reuse leaked credentials to impersonate legitimate users.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Task API's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Task API accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗

  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Task API accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive

  3. Task API accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).

preventive

Log tampering
High STRIDE · Repudiation · Task API (api) CWE-778 standard check
▼
DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Task API (api).

📝Description

An attacker with access modifies or deletes audit trails.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'api' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Task API (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🗺Framework mapping

Trust-boundary crossing without strong authn: Operator → Task API
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Task API (api) CWE-287 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Operator → Task API (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: oauth, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into Application tier. The receiving component is Task API (api).

📝Description

Flow '' crosses trust boundary 'Internet' → 'Application tier'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Operator → Task API crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'Application tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. Task API accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into Task API, terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Cross-boundary input not validated: Operator → Task API
High STRIDE · Tampering · Task API (api) CWE-20 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Operator → Task API (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: oauth, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into Application tier. The receiving component is Task API (api).

📝Description

Data crossing the trust boundary into 'Application tier' must be treated as untrusted, even if the source is internal. Implicit trust is the most common cause of injection / SSRF / deserialization bugs.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Operator → Task API crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'Application tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to Task API.
  2. Sends payloads with unexpected types, lengths, or special characters (e.g. SQL meta-chars, NUL bytes, control sequences).
  3. Task API processes the input under the assumption that the upstream zone has already validated it — but the boundary changes the security context.
  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into Task API or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever Task API does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

🗺Framework mapping

A03

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-20 — Improper Input Validation ↗
Cross-boundary data exposure risk: Operator → Task API
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Task API (api) CWE-200 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk42/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability10/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Operator → Task API (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: oauth, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into Application tier. The receiving component is Task API (api).

📝Description

Information leaving 'Internet' into 'Application tier' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Operator → Task API crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'Application tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Autonomous agent can spawn agents — unbounded consumption: Coder Agent
Medium STRIDE · Denial of Service · Coder Agent (ai_agent) CWE-400 finding
▼
DREAD risk23/50 · Medium
Damage5/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability5/10
Affected users4/10
Discoverability5/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Coder Agent (ai_agent).

📝Description

An autonomous agent that spawns other agents without limits risks runaway loops and cost/resource exhaustion (OWASP LLM10 Unbounded Consumption / Agentic).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Coder Agent'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in Coder Agent (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. Coder Agent's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach Coder Agent; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🗺Framework mapping

A05LLM10T4

🔗References

🔎Why this fired

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Coder Agent via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For ai_agent, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability5/10
Affected users4/10
